slides.h1:
The living museum
from objects to encounters.
slides.lead: Demonstration deck for this template. All text is sample (dummy) content and exists only to show the styles.
slides.notes: Opening slide. Put the event title, a one-line subtitle and a meta row at the bottom. Highlight one word with em for the accent color.
Team slide. It uses initials in a colored circle instead of photos, so it needs no images.

slides.lead: Three parts, with room for questions between them.
slides.notes: Agenda as three large tiles. Good at the start of a block.

slides.lead: The state of the holdings, records and biggest gaps.
slides.notes: Divider slide between blocks. Large heading on a colored gradient.

slides.lead: Objects only matter when people connect with them. Meaning lives in the encounter, not the display case.
slides.footnote: // illustrative slide with an enlarged image
slides.notes: Hero slide: large heading, a bold illustration, a short caption. Good for a point or a chapter opener.

items: more first-time visitors after the new entrance route
longer average dwell time in front of key works
of the collection now catalogued with full provenance
bullets: Measure connection, not footfall: attention beats attendance.
Start with the works you can already tell a story about.
One well-told gallery beats ten rooms of unlabeled objects.
slides.notes: KPI dashboard: three cards with an icon, a big number and a label, supporting bullets below.

slides.lead: An object becomes part of the story step by step.
bullets: Condition checked and photographed on arrival.
Placed where its story lands best.
slides.notes: Two-column slide: bullets with a strong lead word on the left, illustration on the right. Text scales itself.

slides.lead: The same works, two ways to hang them.
cards: Walk through time. Clear, but can feel like a list.
Group by idea. Surprising connections, harder to navigate.
One artist in depth. Rich, but narrow in scope.
Old and new face to face. Fresh, needs strong framing.
slides.notes: A 2x2 grid of cards. Good for comparison or listing options.

slides.lead: Six common building blocks of a modern gallery.
cards: Short, plain text that earns its place beside the work.
A voice that adds context without crowding the eye.
A person who reads the room and adapts the story.
The collection online, open beyond opening hours.
Light, climate and care that keep works alive.
Routes and formats that welcome every visitor.
slides.notes: A 3x2 grid with an icon in each card header. Great for an overview of tools or capabilities.

items: Numbers level off, so the focus shifts to depth and loyalty.
A landmark exhibition pulls in crowds and new audiences fast.
Without renewal, regulars fade and the rooms grow quiet.
slides.notes: Numbered cards with small trend charts and an illustration on the right. Ideal for three possible futures.

slides.lead: Five steps, each with a clear output.
slides.notes: A horizontal flow of steps with arrows. Each box has a heading and a small caption.

slides.quote: A museum is not about the past: it is about choosing what we carry into the future.
slides.by: Sample quote to demonstrate the style
slides.notes: Quote slide with a bold left rule. Keep the attribution small.

bullets: Each room answers a single clear question.
Empty wall is part of the design, not waste.
Let people find their own path through the work.
slides.notes: A plain bullet slide with a strong lead word. Text auto-scales to fill the space.

slides.lead: A gallery without a question becomes a warehouse.
slides.notes: Statement slide: one large sentence centered on the page. Use it for a key message.

slides.lead: What to do this week, this month and this quarter.
slides.notes: Second divider, opening the practical part.

slides.lead: We switch to the floor plan and trace the new visitor route live.
slides.footnote: // placeholder slide for a demo or a break
slides.notes: Placeholder slide. Centered heading, ideal for a live demo, a break or questions.
Closing slide in the style of the title. Contact and a call to action.
